meta.title: Glossary
meta.description: Core terms across the DTC teaching collection. Definitions are teaching-level summaries with links into the guides—not official legal text.

terms.definition: The Depository Trust Company — a central securities depository that holds securities in book-entry form. Designated as a SIFMU under Dodd-Frank.
The Depository Trust & Clearing Corporation — parent holding company of DTC, NSCC, and FICC. Industry-owned and governed.
National Securities Clearing Corporation — provides trade validation, clearing, and CNS netting for equities and corporate debt.
Fixed Income Clearing Corporation — clears U.S. government securities, MBS, and repo transactions through GSD and MBSD.
DTC's partnership nominee. Securities deposited with DTC are registered in the name of Cede & Co. The issuer and trustee typically have no direct knowledge of beneficial owners.
Securities registered in the name of a broker, bank, or nominee (commonly through Cede & Co.) rather than in the name of the beneficial owner.
The person or entity with the economic interest in a security, even when legal title is held by a nominee such as Cede & Co.
International central securities depository (ICSD) headquartered in Brussels. Settles cross-border trades and links to local CSDs and DTCC via bridges and links.
Fast Automated Securities Transfer — DTC program allowing transfer agents to electronically move securities between DTC and the transfer agent without physical certificates.
Direct Registration System — book-entry registration on the issuer's (transfer agent's) books in the investor's name, outside street name at a broker.
Deposit/Withdrawal at Custodian — electronic deposit or withdrawal of securities between a transfer agent and DTC, typically under FAST.
Under FAST, the aggregate position registered to Cede & Co. on the transfer agent’s books representing DTC-held shares of an issue; adjusted electronically rather than reissued for each movement.
Trade date plus one business day. The U.S. standard settlement cycle since May 28, 2024, shortening from T+2 to reduce counterparty risk and free up capital.
Delivery Versus Payment — a settlement mechanism ensuring securities are delivered only if payment is simultaneously made, eliminating principal risk.
Securities issued without physical certificates. Ownership is maintained electronically on DTC's books through programs such as FAST.
Blanket Letter of Representations — an agreement between an issuer, its transfer agent, and DTC covering all (or a series of) securities of that issuer.
Committee on Uniform Securities Identification Procedures — a unique nine-character alphanumeric identifier assigned to securities for clearing and settlement.
SEC safe harbor allowing resale of restricted securities to Qualified Institutional Buyers (QIBs) without registration.
SEC safe harbor for offers and sales of securities outside the United States by domestic and foreign issuers.
Standard Form 28 — Affidavit of Individual Surety used in federal government contracting to verify a surety's assets under FAR 28.203.
Federal Acquisition Regulation section governing individual sureties, including asset verification requirements and acceptance criteria.
Uniform Prudent Management of Institutional Funds Act — governs investment and expenditure decisions of charitable institutions.
Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 — governs investment securities, including security entitlements and rights of entitlement holders and secured parties.
Under UCC Article 8, the rights and property interest of a person who holds a financial asset through a securities intermediary (not a direct claim on a certificate).
Account Control Agreement — a tri-party agreement among a borrower, a secured party (lender), and an intermediary that perfects a security interest in a securities account under UCC Article 8.
The practice whereby a prime broker or lender re-uses collateral posted by a client to finance its own activities or to back its own borrowings.
A written order by one party (drawer) directing another (drawee) to pay a sum to a payee—central to documentary collections and trade finance.
ICC Uniform Rules for Collections — market standard rules governing documentary collections, including bills of exchange presented with shipping documents.
A broker-dealer, bank, or other entity that holds a direct account at DTC and settles through its systems on behalf of itself and customers.
The agent that maintains the issuer's register of holders, processes transfers, and interfaces with DTC under FAST for electronic deposits and withdrawals (DWAC) and DRS.

Fast Automated Securities Transfer — DTC program enabling transfer agents to adjust Cede-registered balance positions electronically instead of printing a new jumbo certificate for each deposit or withdrawal.
Systemically Important Financial Market Utility — designation under Dodd-Frank for critical post-trade infrastructure such as DTC, NSCC, and FICC.
The date used to determine who is entitled to a distribution or certain rights, tested against the registered holder (often Cede & Co. for street-name securities).
The first day a security trades without an upcoming dividend or similar entitlement; settlement conventions align buyers with record-date entitlement.
A proxy covering bulk Cede-held positions so voting authority and beneficial owner instructions can be processed through the intermediary chain.
An event where positions on the relevant date automatically receive the entitlement without a beneficial owner election (e.g., cash dividend, stock split).
An event requiring a beneficial owner or fiduciary election before a deadline (e.g., tender offer, rights exercise); silence often means default or non-participation.
Non-objecting vs objecting beneficial owner status — governs whether a street-name holder’s identity may be disclosed to the issuer for communications.
Temporary transfer of securities inventory (often to settle shorts or fails) against cash or other collateral, for a borrow fee or rebate.
Repurchase agreement — a cash financing trade in which securities are transferred as collateral (commonly documented as sale plus forward repurchase).
Market language for borrowing or lending equity (or other) inventory to obtain deliverable shares, typically with collateral and a fee.
Confirmation that borrow inventory is available before executing a short sale—distinct from the later delivery of shares.
Lender’s demand that borrowed securities be returned, often around corporate actions or inventory needs; borrower must re-source or cover.
